title: RISE with SAP & Cloud Migration
description: RISE with SAP is SAP's flagship offering for migrating on-premise SAP customers to the cloud. Covers RISE business model, S/4HANA Cloud vs private cloud, migration paths, SAP Business Technology Platform integration, clean core strategy, and how to discuss RISE in FAANG interviews as a large-scale migration program.

description.value:
RISE with SAP is not a product — it's a business transformation as a service bundle. Customers subscribe to RISE and receive: S/4HANA Cloud license (private or public edition), infrastructure (hosted by SAP or hyperscaler partners), Business Process Transformation (SAP's methodology), Business Technology Platform (BTP) credits, and SAP's clean core commitment (SAP manages the standard system, customers extend via key user extensibility). The key architectural insight: RISE converts SAP's on-premise license model (perpetual license + maintenance) to a subscription model that includes infrastructure, platform, and transformation services.

RISE vs GROW: RISE with SAP targets existing on-premise SAP customers migrating to cloud. GROW with SAP targets net-new S/4HANA Cloud customers (greenfield). RISE includes a transformation phase (system conversion or migration). GROW includes best-practice templates and rapid deployment. Both include S/4HANA Cloud, BTP credits, and SAP Business AI. From a Staff+ perspective, the difference is migration complexity: RISE deals with existing customizations, data volumes, and integration landscapes; GROW starts clean.

RISE Customer Journey: (1) Assessment — SAP Readiness Check analyzes system for S/4HANA compatibility, custom code usage, and data volumes. (2) Planning — migration path selection (brownfield/system conversion, greenfield/new implementation, selective data transition). (3) Migration — technical migration of S/4HANA, clean core approach (move custom code to BTP extensions), data migration. (4) Operation — SAP manages the infrastructure (Hyperscaler option), customer manages applications and extensions on BTP.

sections.title: Migration Paths: Brownfield vs Greenfield vs Selective

description.value:
Choosing the right migration path is the most consequential decision in a RISE engagement. The choice determines project duration, cost, risk profile, and long-term architectural flexibility. Three paths exist, and the Staff+ architect must evaluate which path aligns with the customer's business constraints.

Brownfield (System Conversion): Convert the existing ECC system to S/4HANA in-place. All customizations, configurations, and data are carried forward. Fastest path — a small ECC system can convert in a weekend. Lowest business disruption — users see the same transactions with an updated UI. Highest technical risk — existing customizations that worked on ECC may break on S/4HANA. Best for: systems with low custom code volume, no major process redesign needed, time-constrained migrations (end-of-maintenance deadlines).

Greenfield (New Implementation): Build a new S/4HANA system from scratch using SAP Best Practices and re-implement business processes. Cleanest architecture — no legacy customizations, no technical debt, full clean core. Highest business disruption — requires business process re-engineering, data migration from old system. Best for: organizations that want to re-engineer processes, have complex customizations that are not worth carrying forward, or are adopting SAP for the first time.

Selective Data Transition (SDT): Migrate selected data (master data, open transactions, historical data) from ECC to a new S/4HANA system while re-implementing processes. Combines aspects of both: new S/4HANA system with clean core, but selected data migrated to preserve business continuity. Best for: hybrid scenarios where some processes need redesign but master data is too valuable to re-enter.

decisionTree.prompt: Which migration path should this RISE customer choose?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "How much custom code does the current ECC system have?" <20% modification rate, low custom code = brownfield. >40% modification rate with heavy customizations = greenfield. Medium modification with valuable master data = selective data transition.

branches.condition: Low custom code (<20%), end-of-maintenance deadline within 12 months, no major process redesign desired
branches.path: Brownfield (System Conversion). Fastest (3-6 months), lowest cost, least disruption. Run Readiness Check 2.0 to identify compatibility issues. Use the SUM tool for the conversion. Post-migration: gradually move extensions to BTP for long-term clean core.

branches.condition: High custom code (>40%), heavy modifications to standard, business wants process redesign
branches.path: Greenfield (New Implementation). 12-18 months. Re-implement processes using SAP Best Practices. Migrate only master data and open transactions. Clean core from day one. Build extensions on BTP using RAP/CAP.

branches.condition: Medium custom code, valuable master data (10+ years of customer/material/vendor data), some processes need redesign
branches.path: Selective Data Transition. 9-15 months. Use SAP SDT tools (Migration Cockpit). Migrate master data + selected transactions. Re-implement redesigned processes. Clean core with selectively carried-forward data.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: The best Staff+ answer combines paths per domain: 'We used brownfield for Finance (low custom code, high data volume), greenfield for Supply Chain (process redesign needed), and selective data transition for Master Data (10 years of customer data that cannot be re-entered).' Polyglot migration strategies demonstrate architectural thinking beyond product knowledge.

sections.title: Clean Core & BTP Extension Strategy

description.value:
The clean core principle is central to RISE: keep the S/4HANA system as standard as possible (no custom ABAP in Z-tables, no modified SAP standard objects) and move all customizations to side-by-side extensions on SAP BTP. This is not just an SAP best practice — it's an architectural requirement. SAP manages quarterly updates for RISE customers, and custom code in the core creates update conflicts.

What Goes on BTP: Custom business logic (RAP applications, CAP services). Custom UIs (Fiori apps deployed to BTP). Integrations (Cloud Integration, Event Mesh). Analytics (Datasphere + SAC). AI/ML (AI Foundation services). Key user extensibility (in-app extensibility for simple field additions remains in S/4HANA).

Released APIs: Clean core means you only use released APIs — SAP's stable, versioned APIs that do not break during upgrades. The SAP API Business Hub catalogs released APIs for OData, SOAP, and RFC. If an API is not marked as released, it may change without notice — using it violates the clean core principle. This is the single most important operational rule for RISE customers.

Extension Strategy Governance: (1) Every extension request must answer: Can this be achieved with key user extensibility (in-app)? If yes, do it in S/4HANA. If no, build a side-by-side extension on BTP. (2) Side-by-side extensions must use released APIs only. (3) Extensions must be deployed via CI/CD pipelines (not transported). (4) Each extension must have an owner and a lifecycle plan.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "Our team has 200 custom Z-programs that power critical business processes. The clean core strategy means we rewrite all of them for BTP. That's a 2-year project with no new features during migration. How do you justify this to the CFO?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Clean core is SAP's strategy. We must follow it." (no business justification, no empathy for the cost)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "Let me reframe the cost-benefit. Not rewriting all 200 at once — we prioritize by upgrade impact. Here's the key insight: every quarter, SAP applies updates to RISE S/4HANA systems. Each Z-program that touches a modified standard object creates a regression testing cost. Our analysis shows 60% of the 200 programs have NOT been executed in 2+ years (usage statistics from SAP). We deprecate those first — zero rewrite cost, immediate upgrade risk reduction. Of the remaining 80 programs, 50 use released APIs and can be moved to BTP incrementally — 5 per quarter over 10 quarters. The remaining 30 use unreleased APIs and need redesign. The CFO sees: (1) Year 1 cost: $150K for 10 BTP extensions + $0 for 120 deprecated programs. (2) Year 1 benefit: upgrade testing savings of $200K (no regression testing on deprecated programs), reduced upgrade conflicts. (3) Year 2-3: remaining 70 programs migrated. Avoided upgrade costs offset migration costs by Year 3. The long-term benefit: quarterly upgrades go from 4-week testing windows to 1-week testing windows — a 75% reduction in upgrade effort. This is not a cost — it's an investment with 3-year payback."
adversarialResponse.pattern: quantify-and-prioritize — don't demand a full rewrite. Deprecate unused programs first (60% of the problem solved for $0). Move the remainder incrementally. Show the financial model: migration cost vs avoided upgrade testing cost. The Staff+ insight: clean core is not a philosophical position — it's an operational cost reduction with measurable ROI.

description.value:
RISE includes BTP credits (typically 10-15% of the RISE contract value) for building integrations, extensions, and analytics. These credits are consumed by Cloud Integration (CPI), Event Mesh, Datasphere, AI Foundation, and CAP/Steampunk runtimes. Understanding how BTP credits are consumed and how to optimize usage is a key Staff+ skill.

Integration Scenarios: Cloud Integration (CPI) handles ~80% of RISE integration scenarios: connecting S/4HANA Cloud to third-party systems (Salesforce, Workday, banks, logistics providers). Event Mesh connects S/4HANA Cloud events to custom consumers on BTP. Open Connectors provides pre-built connectors for 200+ third-party applications. API Management exposes S/4HANA APIs through a developer portal with rate limiting, security policies, and usage analytics.

BTP Credit Optimization: BTP credits cover runtime consumption (vCPU/memory for CAP apps), Cloud Integration messages, Datasphere storage, and AI Foundation service calls. The most common optimization failure: over-provisioning CAP runtimes for low-traffic extensions. Use auto-scaling (set min=0 for non-production) to minimize credit consumption. Monitor credit usage monthly — BTP credits that are not consumed in the contract year are forfeited.

RISE with SAP appears in FAANG interviews when interviewers ask about large-scale migration programs. The framing question is typically: 'Tell me about a time you managed a complex migration or platform transformation.' RISE is an excellent story because it combines technical migration, business model transformation, organizational change, and clean core strategy — all Staff+ signals.

How to Use RISE as a STAR Story: Situation — Our company was running SAP ECC 6.0 with 15 years of customizations (1,200 Z-programs). SAP announced end-of-maintenance for ECC 6.0 by 2027. The board mandated a cloud migration. Task — I led the technical assessment and migration strategy for a RISE with SAP engagement covering 30 countries, 12,000 users, and 200+ integrations. Action — I conducted the Readiness Check, identified that 60% of custom code was unused, designed a clean core strategy that deprecated 800 programs and migrated 400 to BTP. I selected selective data transition for master data (preserved 10 years of customer data) and brownfield for Finance (5 custom programs only). Result — Cloud migration completed in 14 months (4 months ahead of schedule). Maintenance costs reduced by 40%. Quarterly upgrades became 1 week instead of 4 weeks. Upgrade conflicts dropped by 90%.

adversarialResponse.challenge: "RISE is just a commercial bundle — SAP wrapping the same products in a subscription model. There's no technical innovation. Why should I care about this as an engineer?"
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "RISE is SAP's strategic direction and all customers must adopt it." (defensive, no technical substance)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You're right that RISE is commercial packaging. But the packaging forces architectural constraints that are genuinely innovative. Before RISE, SAP customers could defer upgrades indefinitely (the average ECC system was on a 7-year-old release). The clean core requirement in RISE forces customers to adopt a modern cloud-native architecture: side-by-side extensions, released APIs, CI/CD deployment, and quarterly upgrades. This is the same architectural transformation that drove Netflix from datacenters to AWS, or that drove Microsoft from on-prem Exchange to Office 365. The technical innovation is not in the RISE contract — it's in the architectural constraints that the contract enforces. Before RISE, there was no business incentive for an SAP customer to adopt clean core. After RISE, the incentive is clear: quarterly upgrades that don't break. As an engineer, I care about RISE because it creates the business conditions for architectural modernization that I've been advocating for years."
adversarialResponse.pattern: reframe-constraints-as-innovation — acknowledge the commercial criticism, then explain how business constraints drive architectural innovation. Connect to industry parallels (Netflix, Microsoft). The Staff+ insight: the best architecture is sometimes forced by business constraints, not chosen freely.

details: Staff+ Insight: RISE with SAP is the most consequential transformation in SAP's history. It converts SAP's business model from perpetual licenses (which incentivize customization) to a subscription service (which incentivizes standardization). The clean core architectural pattern — keep S/4HANA standard, extend via BTP — is the same separation of concerns that drives microservices architecture. Staff+ engineers who understand this architectural parallel can lead RISE migrations as platform transformations, not just technical upgrades.
